# automaticstay.org -- Full Content

This file contains the full text content of all pages on automaticstay.org.
Part of the Open Bankruptcy Project (openbankruptcyproject.org).


---

## Automatic Stay: Business vs Personal Bankruptcy - 362 Stay
Source: https://automaticstay.org/business-vs-personal.html

Skip to main content

automaticstay.org
☰

Home
What It Stops
Violations
Repeat Filers
Relief from Stay
Exceptions
Duration
Stay Exceptions
Business vs Personal

Business vs Personal Automatic Stay

The same Section 362 applies to both, but business cases have unique considerations

Home › Business vs Personal

Same Statute, Different Application

The automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362(a) applies the moment any bankruptcy petition is filed - whether it is filed by an individual, a corporation, an LLC, or a partnership. The same statutory protections apply regardless of the debtor's entity type.

However, the practical effects can differ significantly between personal and business bankruptcies.

What the Stay Protects in Business Cases

When a business entity files bankruptcy, the stay protects:

All property of the bankruptcy estate (inventory, equipment, accounts receivable, real property)

The business entity itself from lawsuits and collection

Business bank accounts from freezing or setoff (with some exceptions for financial institutions)

Leased property from termination (with conditions)

What the Stay Does NOT Protect

Guarantors and Co-Obligors

In personal Chapter 7 or Chapter 11, the stay protects the debtor but not co-debtors or guarantors. If a business owner personally guaranteed a business loan, creditors can pursue the personal guaranty even while the business is in bankruptcy - unless the individual also files.

Exception: In Chapter 13, the co-debtor stay under Section 1301 protects individuals who are co-liable on consumer debts with the debtor. But this only applies to consumer debts of individuals, not business obligations.

Partners in a Partnership Case

When a partnership files bankruptcy, the stay does not automatically protect the individual partners. Creditors can pursue the partners individually on partnership debts unless the partners also file their own bankruptcy cases.

LLCs and the Automatic Stay

When an LLC files bankruptcy, the stay protects the LLC's assets but not the personal assets of the LLC's members. If members have personal exposure (for example, through personal guarantees, pierced corporate veils, or fraud), creditors can pursue the members individually.

Conversely, when an individual member files personal bankruptcy, the stay does not protect the LLC or its assets (since the LLC is a separate legal entity). The debtor's membership interest in the LLC is part of the bankruptcy estate, but the LLC itself is not in bankruptcy.

Sole Proprietorships: No Distinction

A sole proprietorship is not a separate legal entity from its owner. When the owner files personal bankruptcy, the stay protects both personal and business assets equally. All business debts are personal debts. All business assets are the debtor's assets. There is no distinction between the business and the individual.

Cross-References

automaticstay.org - Comprehensive Section 362 guide

relieffromstay.org - Relief from stay for secured creditors

codebtorstay.org - Co-debtor stay in Chapter 13

serialfiler.org - Stay limitations for repeat filers

## How Long Does the Automatic Stay Last? - 362 Stay
Source: https://automaticstay.org/duration.html

Skip to main content

automaticstay.org
☰

Home
What It Stops
Violations
Repeat Filers
Relief from Stay
Exceptions
Duration
Stay Exceptions
Business vs Personal

How Long Does the Automatic Stay Last?

Duration of the stay for first-time and repeat filers, and how the court can lift it early

Home › Duration

First-Time Filers: Stay Lasts the Entire Case

If you have not had a bankruptcy case dismissed within the past year, the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362(a) remains in effect for the duration of your bankruptcy case. That means:

Chapter 7: The stay lasts until discharge (typically 3-4 months) or until the case is dismissed or closed

Chapter 13: The stay lasts for the entire plan period (3-5 years), until discharge, dismissal, or closing

Chapter 11: The stay lasts until the plan is confirmed and the case is closed or converted

The stay terminates automatically with respect to the debtor's property when the case is closed, dismissed, or the debtor receives a discharge. It terminates with respect to estate property when that property is no longer part of the estate.

Repeat Filers: Reduced or No Stay

One dismissal in the past year - 362(c)(3)

If you had a bankruptcy case pending within the preceding one year that was dismissed, the automatic stay in your new case terminates after 30 days unless you file a motion to extend it. The court will extend the stay only if you demonstrate that the new case was filed in good faith.

The 30-day deadline is strict. If you miss it, the stay expires and creditors can resume collection. File the extension motion immediately upon filing the new case.

Two or more dismissals in the past year - 362(c)(4)

If you had two or more cases pending within the preceding year that were dismissed, the automatic stay does not go into effect at all when you file a new case. You must file a motion asking the court to impose the stay, and prove that the new filing is in good faith.

Good faith factors: Courts consider whether you are addressing the issues that led to prior dismissals, whether your financial circumstances have changed, whether you are likely to complete the new case, and whether the filing appears to be a tactic to delay creditors.

When the Court Can Lift the Stay Early

Even in a first-time filing, creditors can file a motion for relief from stay under Section 362(d). The court may grant relief if:

Cause exists - including lack of adequate protection of the creditor's interest in property (Section 362(d)(1))

No equity + not necessary for reorganization - the debtor has no equity in the property and it is not necessary for an effective reorganization (Section 362(d)(2))

Single asset real estate - special rules under Section 362(d)(3)

If the court grants relief from stay, the stay is lifted only as to that specific creditor and that specific property. The stay remains in effect against all other creditors.

Cross-References

automaticstay.org - Comprehensive guide to Section 362

relieffromstay.org - Relief from stay motions explained

serialfiler.org - Special rules for repeat filers

## Exceptions to the Automatic Stay: Section 362(b) - 362 Stay
Source: https://automaticstay.org/exceptions.html

Skip to main content

automaticstay.org
☰

Home
What It Stops
Violations
Repeat Filers
Relief from Stay
Exceptions

Exceptions to the Automatic Stay

Section 362(b) lists the actions that are NOT stopped by the automatic stay. These exceptions allow certain proceedings to continue despite the bankruptcy filing.

Home›Exceptions

Overview

While the automatic stay under Section 362(a) is one of the broadest injunctions in federal law, it is not absolute. Section 362(b) currently contains over 25 specific exceptions. Below are the exceptions most commonly encountered by consumer and small business debtors, grouped by category.

Criminal and Government Actions

Criminal Proceedings

Section 362(b)(1)

The commencement or continuation of a criminal action or proceeding against the debtor. Bankruptcy does not shield anyone from criminal prosecution. This includes pending criminal cases, new indictments, probation revocation, and sentencing proceedings.

Government Police and Regulatory Power

Section 362(b)(4)

Actions by a governmental unit to enforce its police or regulatory power. This includes environmental enforcement (EPA cleanup orders), building code enforcement, health and safety inspections, professional licensing proceedings, and consumer protection enforcement. The key question is whether the government is acting to protect the public interest (excepted) or to collect money (stayed).

Domestic and Family

Domestic Support Obligations

Section 362(b)(2)

Collection of domestic support obligations (child support and alimony) from property that is not property of the estate. Wage withholding for current support continues. Paternity actions, support modification actions, and divorce proceedings related to support can proceed. This is one of the most important exceptions for family law practitioners.

Divorce and Dissolution

Section 362(b)(2)(A)(ii)-(iv)

The establishment of paternity, the establishment or modification of domestic support orders, and proceedings concerning child custody or visitation are all excepted from the stay. The divorce itself can usually proceed, but division of marital property that is property of the estate may still be stayed. Courts draw a distinction between the divorce decree and the property division.

Tax-Related Exceptions

Tax Audits and Assessments

Section 362(b)(9)

An audit by a governmental unit to determine tax liability, the issuance of a notice of tax deficiency, a demand for tax returns, and the making of an assessment and issuance of a demand for payment of such assessment are all excepted. However, actual collection activity - levies, seizures of property, garnishments - remains stayed. The distinction is between determining how much is owed (allowed) and collecting it (stayed).

Tax Court Proceedings

Section 362(b)(9)

The commencement or continuation of a proceeding before the United States Tax Court is excepted from the stay. Debtors can still contest tax liability in Tax Court during bankruptcy.

Real Property and Eviction

Eviction with Pre-Petition Judgment

Section 362(b)(22)

If the landlord obtained a judgment for possession of the debtor's residential property before the bankruptcy petition was filed, the landlord may continue the eviction. The debtor has a narrow right to delay under Section 362(l) by filing a certification (within 30 days) that the debtor can cure the default under state law, along with a deposit of one month's rent. But this protection is limited and temporary.

Eviction Based on Endangerment or Illegal Drug Use

Section 362(b)(23)

If the eviction is based on endangerment of the property or illegal use of controlled substances on the property, the landlord may proceed regardless of the filing. The debtor cannot use the certification process under Section 362(l) to delay evictions on these grounds.

Financial and Securities

Setoff by Commodity or Securities Broker

Section 362(b)(6), (b)(7), (b)(17), (b)(27)

Various exceptions protect the ability of financial institutions, commodity brokers, forward contract merchants, swap participants, and securities clearing agencies to exercise contractual rights of setoff and to close out and net positions. These exceptions protect the stability of financial markets.

Presentment of Negotiable Instruments

Section 362(b)(11)

The presentment of a negotiable instrument and the giving of notice of and protesting dishonor of such an instrument is excepted. This allows checks to be processed and negotiable instruments to clear through the banking system.

Other Notable Exceptions

ERISA and Pension Loans

Section 362(b)(19)

Withholding of income from the debtor's wages to repay a loan from an ERISA-qualified pension, profit-sharing, or stock bonus plan is excepted from the stay, provided the loan was made before the filing.

Acts to Perfect Interests Within Statutory Grace Periods

Section 362(b)(3)

A creditor may perfect a security interest (such as recording a lien) within the time permitted under Sections 546(b) and 547(e)(2)(A). This prevents the automatic stay from creating a gap that turns properly perfected liens into avoidable preferences.

HUD-Insured Mortgage Foreclosure (Section 362(b)(8))

Section 362(b)(8)

Certain actions by the Secretary of Housing and Urban Development to foreclose a mortgage or deed of trust insured under the National Housing Act are excepted if the mortgage is held by HUD or insured under a specific HUD program.

The full text of Section 362(b) contains additional exceptions for specialized situations including student loan proceedings under certain conditions, securities regulatory actions, and more. For the complete statutory text, see 11 U.S.C. Section 362(b) on Cornell LII.

Related pages: Automatic Stay Overview - What the Stay Stops - Stay Violations - Relief from Stay Motions - Repeat Filer Limits

## Automatic Stay in Bankruptcy - Section 362 Guide [2026] - 362 Stay
Source: https://automaticstay.org/

Skip to main content

automaticstay.org
☰

Home
What It Stops
Violations
Repeat Filers
Relief from Stay
Exceptions

Part of the Open Bankruptcy Project - 161+ free domains, 115,000+ pages, $0 cost

Automatic Stay in Bankruptcy

A plain-language guide to 11 U.S.C. Section 362 - the federal law that stops creditors in their tracks the moment you file for bankruptcy.

What Is the Automatic Stay?

The automatic stay is one of the most powerful protections in federal law. The moment a bankruptcy petition is filed - whether Chapter 7, Chapter 11, Chapter 12, or Chapter 13 - an immediate, court-ordered injunction takes effect against virtually all creditor activity. No separate motion is required. No judge needs to sign an order. It is automatic.

The legal authority comes from 11 U.S.C. Section 362. Subsection (a) lists the specific acts that are stayed, and subsection (b) lists the exceptions. The stay applies to actions against the debtor personally and against property of the bankruptcy estate.

For most people filing bankruptcy, the automatic stay is the first tangible relief they experience. Foreclosure sales are halted. Repossession agents must stop. Wage garnishments cease. Lawsuits are frozen. Collection calls must end. This breathing room is the foundation that makes bankruptcy reorganization - or an orderly liquidation - possible.

When Does the Stay Take Effect?

The automatic stay takes effect at the exact moment the bankruptcy petition is filed with the clerk of the bankruptcy court. In the era of electronic filing, this means the precise date and time stamp on the petition. Creditors who take action after that moment - even if they did not yet know about the filing - are in violation of the stay.

The filing creates a presumption of validity. Even if the petition is later dismissed, any creditor action taken while the stay was in effect is void or voidable. Courts have consistently held that creditors have an affirmative duty to reverse actions taken in violation of the stay, including returning repossessed vehicles, reversing garnishments, and vacating foreclosure sales.

Who Does the Stay Protect?

The stay protects the debtor (the person or entity that filed), property of the debtor, and property of the bankruptcy estate. In Chapter 13 cases, the stay extends additional protections to co-debtors on consumer debts under 11 U.S.C. Section 1301 - meaning a creditor cannot go after a co-signer while the Chapter 13 plan is in effect.

The stay does not generally protect non-filing co-debtors in Chapter 7 cases, guarantors on business debts, or separate legal entities. If a debtor is an individual who owns a corporation, the automatic stay protects the individual but does not automatically extend to the corporation (unless the corporation also filed).

What the Automatic Stay Stops

Section 362(a) lists the actions that are stayed. In practical terms, this includes:

Foreclosure - Mortgage lenders must halt foreclosure proceedings, including scheduled sales

Repossession - Auto lenders and other secured creditors cannot repossess collateral

Wage garnishment - Employers must stop withholding wages for creditor judgments

Lawsuits - Pending litigation against the debtor is frozen; new suits cannot be filed

Collection calls and letters - All collection activity must cease immediately

Utility disconnections - Utilities cannot shut off service for the first 20 days (with conditions)

Bank account levies - Creditors cannot freeze or seize bank accounts

Tax liens - The IRS and state tax authorities cannot file new liens or continue levy actions

Eviction - In some circumstances, eviction proceedings are paused (with limitations)

Setoff - Creditors cannot offset mutual debts without court permission

For a detailed breakdown of each category, see What the Automatic Stay Stops.

What the Stay Does NOT Stop

The automatic stay is broad, but it is not absolute. Section 362(b) carves out over 25 exceptions. The most commonly encountered include:

Criminal proceedings - The government can prosecute criminal cases regardless of the filing

Domestic support obligations - Child support and alimony collection continues

Certain tax proceedings - Tax audits and assessments may continue

Evictions with prior judgment - If the landlord already has a judgment for possession before the filing, eviction may proceed

Police and regulatory power - Government enforcement actions for public health and safety are not stayed

See the full list at Exceptions to the Automatic Stay.

When the Stay Disappears or Is Limited

Warning for repeat filers: If you filed a previous bankruptcy case that was dismissed within the past year, you may receive only 30 days of stay protection - or no automatic stay at all.

Second Filing Within One Year - Section 362(c)(3)

If a debtor had one prior case pending within the preceding one-year period and that case was dismissed, the automatic stay in the new case expires after 30 days unless the debtor files a motion and the court extends it. The debtor must prove the new case was filed in good faith. This is common when a Chapter 13 case is dismissed for missed plan payments and the debtor refiles.

Third or More Filings Within One Year - Section 362(c)(4)

If the debtor had two or more prior cases pending within the preceding one-year period and those cases were dismissed, the new filing does not create any automatic stay at all. The debtor must affirmatively ask the court to impose a stay and demonstrate good faith. Until the court acts, creditors may proceed as if no bankruptcy was filed.

For the complete breakdown of repeat filer rules, see Automatic Stay Limits for Repeat Filers.

Creditor Violations and Your Remedies

When a creditor violates the automatic stay, they are acting in contempt of a federal court order. Under 11 U.S.C. Section 362(k), an individual debtor injured by a willful violation can recover:

Actual damages (including out-of-pocket costs, lost wages, and emotional distress in some circuits)

Costs and attorney fees incurred in enforcing the stay

Punitive damages in appropriate circumstances

Common violations include continuing collection calls after notice of the filing, repossessing a vehicle after the petition date, proceeding with a foreclosure sale, or garnishing wages after the employer has been notified. The creditor's lack of knowledge of the filing is not a defense to the automatic nature of the stay, although it may be relevant to whether the violation was "willful."

Learn how to document violations and seek damages at Creditor Violated the Automatic Stay?

Motions for Relief from Stay

Creditors are not powerless against the stay. Any party in interest can file a motion for relief from stay under Section 362(d), asking the court to lift the stay so the creditor can proceed with its rights - typically foreclosure on real property or repossession of a vehicle.

The court will grant relief for "cause" (which includes lack of adequate protection of the creditor's interest) or if the debtor has no equity in the property and the property is not necessary for an effective reorganization. The court must rule within 30 days of a request for a final hearing, or relief may be granted by operation of law.

For details on how these motions work and how to respond, see Motions for Relief from Stay.

Explore This Guide

What the Stay Stops

Complete list of creditor actions halted by the automatic stay, including foreclosure, repossession, garnishment, and more.

Stay Violations

How to document violations, file a motion for contempt, and recover damages under Section 362(k).

Repeat Filer Limits

The 30-day stay termination and no-stay rules under Sections 362(c)(3) and 362(c)(4).

Relief from Stay

When creditors ask the court to lift the stay, what grounds they need, and how to oppose.

Exceptions

The full list of actions not covered by the automatic stay under Section 362(b).

Discharge Screener

Free tool to check whether Section 1328(f) bars a Chapter 13 discharge based on prior filings.

Frequently Asked Questions

What does the automatic stay stop?

The automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362(a) stops foreclosures, repossessions, wage garnishments, lawsuits, collection calls, utility shutoffs, and most other creditor actions against the debtor or the debtor's property. It takes effect immediately when the bankruptcy petition is filed.

How long does the automatic stay last?

For first-time filers, the automatic stay lasts for the duration of the bankruptcy case - typically 3-5 years in Chapter 13 or 3-6 months in Chapter 7. For repeat filers, special limits apply under Sections 362(c)(3) and 362(c)(4).

Can creditors get around the automatic stay?

Yes. Creditors can file a motion for relief from stay asking the court to allow them to proceed with foreclosure, repossession, or other collection actions. The court will grant relief if the creditor shows cause, such as lack of adequate protection, or that the debtor has no equity in the property and it is not necessary for reorganization.

What if a creditor violates the automatic stay?

Under 11 U.S.C. Section 362(k), an individual debtor injured by a willful violation of the automatic stay can recover actual damages, costs, attorney fees, and in appropriate circumstances, punitive damages. The debtor must file a motion for contempt in the bankruptcy court.

Does the automatic stay apply to all debts?

No. Section 362(b) lists over 25 exceptions. Criminal proceedings, domestic support obligations (child support and alimony), certain tax proceedings, and some eviction actions are not stopped by the automatic stay. Additionally, repeat filers may receive reduced or no stay protection under Sections 362(c)(3) and 362(c)(4).

Legal References

11 U.S.C. Section 362 - Automatic stay

11 U.S.C. Section 361 - Adequate protection

11 U.S.C. Section 1301 - Stay of action against co-debtor (Chapter 13)

11 U.S.C. Section 362(k) - Damages for willful violations

11 U.S.C. Section 105(a) - Court's general power to enforce the stay

Section 362 Overview on 1328f.com - Additional context and research data

## Motions for Relief from Stay: When Creditors Ask to Proceed - 362 Stay
Source: https://automaticstay.org/relief-from-stay.html

Skip to main content

automaticstay.org
☰

Home
What It Stops
Violations
Repeat Filers
Relief from Stay
Exceptions

Motions for Relief from Stay

When creditors ask the bankruptcy court to lift the automatic stay so they can proceed with foreclosure, repossession, or other collection.

Home›Relief from Stay

What Is a Motion for Relief from Stay?

A motion for relief from stay (sometimes called a "lift stay motion" or "MFR") is a request by a creditor asking the bankruptcy court to modify, condition, or terminate the automatic stay so the creditor can exercise its rights outside of bankruptcy. This is the creditor's primary tool for getting around the stay without violating it.

The legal authority is 11 U.S.C. Section 362(d), which provides four grounds for relief. The motion is filed in the bankruptcy court, the debtor has the right to respond and be heard, and the court must act within strict time limits.

Grounds for Relief

Section 362(d) provides four separate grounds. A creditor needs to establish only one.

362(d)(1): For Cause, Including Lack of Adequate Protection

The most common ground. "Cause" is not defined in the statute and is determined on a case-by-case basis. The most frequently litigated form of cause is lack of adequate protection - when the creditor's interest in collateral is declining in value and the debtor is not making payments or otherwise protecting the creditor's position. For example, if a homeowner has stopped making mortgage payments and the property is declining in value, the lender can argue that its security interest is not adequately protected.

Other forms of "cause" include: the debtor has no intention of reorganizing, the property is not insured, or the debtor is wasting the collateral.

362(d)(2): No Equity and Not Necessary for Reorganization

Relief is granted if the debtor has no equity in the property AND the property is not necessary for an effective reorganization. Both elements must be met. This ground is commonly used for underwater properties - where the debt exceeds the value of the collateral. In Chapter 7 cases, property is rarely "necessary for reorganization," making this ground easier to establish. In Chapter 13 cases, the debtor can argue that a vehicle or home is necessary for the reorganization plan.

362(d)(3): Single Asset Real Estate

For "single asset real estate" cases (a debtor whose primary asset is a single piece of real property generating income), the creditor can get relief if the debtor has not filed a reorganization plan within 90 days of the order for relief or started making interest payments to the creditor. This provision prevents investors from using bankruptcy to delay foreclosure on rental or commercial property without a genuine reorganization effort.

362(d)(4): Scheme to Delay, Hinder, or Defraud

Relief is granted if the court finds that the filing is part of a scheme to delay, hinder, or defraud creditors involving either a transfer of property without consent of the secured creditor or multiple bankruptcy filings affecting the same property. If relief is granted under this subsection, it can be made in rem - meaning it attaches to the property itself and prevents future filings from staying enforcement against that property for two years.

Timeline and Procedure

Motion Filed

The creditor files the motion and serves it on the debtor, debtor's attorney, the trustee, and any committee. A filing fee is required (currently $181 in most districts).

30 Days: Preliminary Hearing or Final Hearing

Under Section 362(e)(1), the court must commence a final hearing within 30 days of the motion being filed. If the final hearing is not commenced within 30 days, the stay is automatically terminated against the movant. The court can continue the preliminary hearing for up to 30 additional days for cause, but only if the stay remains in effect during the continuance.

Response Deadline

The debtor typically has 14 days to respond (check local rules - some districts allow more or less time). A failure to respond may result in the motion being granted by default.

Hearing and Order

If the debtor opposes, the court holds a hearing. The burden of proof on equity issues falls on the debtor. The burden on all other issues falls on the creditor. The court may grant, deny, or condition relief (e.g., granting relief only if the debtor fails to make adequate protection payments going forward).

Do not ignore a motion for relief from stay. If you fail to respond and appear, the court may grant the motion by default. Once the stay is lifted, the creditor can proceed immediately with foreclosure, repossession, or other collection - and you lose the protection that makes bankruptcy effective.

How to Oppose a Motion for Relief from Stay

If you receive a motion for relief from stay, you need to act quickly. Here are the key strategies for opposing:

Show Adequate Protection

If the creditor argues lack of adequate protection, demonstrate that you are making payments (or propose to start), the property is insured, and the creditor's equity cushion is sufficient. Adequate protection can take the form of periodic cash payments, an additional lien on other property, or other relief that gives the creditor the "indubitable equivalent" of its interest.

Demonstrate Equity

If the creditor argues no equity under 362(d)(2), present evidence that the property is worth more than the debt - through appraisals, comparable sales, or tax assessments. Even a small amount of equity can defeat this prong.

Prove Necessity for Reorganization

In Chapter 13 cases, argue that the property is necessary for your reorganization - a vehicle you need for work, a home that provides housing, or business equipment needed to generate income for plan payments. You must have a viable plan that proposes to cure the arrearage and maintain ongoing payments.

Propose Conditions

Courts often grant conditional relief rather than outright termination. You can propose conditions: "Deny the motion so long as the debtor makes monthly adequate protection payments of $X beginning on [date]." This approach keeps the stay in place while protecting the creditor's interest going forward.

Effect of Relief from Stay

When the court grants relief from stay, the creditor is authorized to proceed with its state-law remedies as if the bankruptcy had not been filed - but only with respect to the specific property or action covered by the order. The stay remains in effect as to all other creditors and all other property.

Relief from stay does not discharge the debt, forgive the obligation, or affect the debtor's right to object to the creditor's claim in the bankruptcy case. It simply removes the stay's injunction with respect to that specific creditor and that specific collateral.

If relief is granted and the creditor forecloses or repossesses, the debtor may still have a claim for any deficiency or surplus, depending on state law and the terms of the bankruptcy plan.

Related pages: Automatic Stay Overview - What the Stay Stops - Stay Violations and Damages - Repeat Filer Limits

Further Reading & Resources

Authority sources for deeper research on the automatic stay:

Cornell LII - the statute that creates the automatic stay

11 U.S.C. Section 1301
Cornell LII - codebtor stay in Chapter 13

## Automatic Stay Limits for Repeat Filers: 362(c)(3) and 362(c)(4) - 362 Stay
Source: https://automaticstay.org/serial-filer-limits.html

Skip to main content

automaticstay.org
☰

Home
What It Stops
Violations
Repeat Filers
Relief from Stay
Exceptions

Automatic Stay Limits for Repeat Filers

Sections 362(c)(3) and 362(c)(4) restrict or eliminate automatic stay protection when a debtor has had recent dismissed cases.

Home›Repeat Filer Limits

Why Repeat Filers Get Less Protection

Before 2005, every bankruptcy filing triggered a full automatic stay regardless of how many prior cases the debtor had filed. Congress changed this with the Bankruptcy Abuse Prevention and Consumer Protection Act (BAPCPA), adding Sections 362(c)(3) and 362(c)(4) to address serial filings - cases filed primarily to invoke the stay without intending to complete the bankruptcy process.

The concern was that some debtors (or their attorneys) were filing bankruptcy petitions solely to stop foreclosure sales or repossessions, allowing the case to be dismissed, and then refiling repeatedly. Each filing would trigger a new stay, potentially delaying creditors for months or years without any real reorganization effort.

The result is a two-tier system based on how many dismissed cases the debtor has had in the preceding year.

Section 362(c)(3)

Trigger
1 prior case dismissed in past year
Stay Duration
30 days (then expires)
Remedy
Motion to extend stay

Section 362(c)(4)

Trigger
2+ prior cases dismissed in past year
Stay Duration
No stay at all
Remedy
Motion to impose stay

Section 362(c)(3): The 30-Day Stay

If a debtor had one prior bankruptcy case that was pending within the preceding one-year period and that case was dismissed, the automatic stay in the new case terminates after 30 days with respect to the debtor (and in some courts, with respect to property of the estate) unless the court extends it.

What "Pending Within the Preceding One-Year Period" Means

The one-year lookback is measured from the new filing date. The prior case must have been pending (open) at any point during that year and then dismissed. It does not matter when the prior case was originally filed - only that it was pending and dismissed within the year before the new filing.

How to Extend the Stay

The debtor must file a motion to extend the stay before the 30 days expire. The motion must be set for hearing within the 30-day period. The debtor must demonstrate by clear and convincing evidence that the new case was filed in good faith.

There is a rebuttable presumption that the case was NOT filed in good faith if:

The prior case was dismissed after the debtor failed to file required documents (schedules, plan, etc.)

The prior case was dismissed after the debtor failed to perform the terms of a confirmed plan

There has been no substantial change in the debtor's financial or personal circumstances since the prior dismissal

To overcome this presumption, the debtor typically must show that the reason for the prior dismissal has been resolved - for example, a new source of income, resolution of a dispute that disrupted plan payments, or a changed financial situation that makes the new case viable.

Critical timing: The motion must be filed and heard within 30 days of the petition date. If the debtor misses this window, the stay terminates by operation of law. Some courts will not grant extensions after the 30 days have passed. File the motion immediately upon filing the new case.

Section 362(c)(4): No Stay at All

If the debtor had two or more prior cases that were pending within the preceding one-year period and were dismissed, the new filing does not trigger any automatic stay. Creditors may proceed with foreclosure, repossession, and collection actions as if no bankruptcy was filed.

How to Get the Stay Imposed

The debtor must file a motion asking the court to impose the stay. The court may impose the stay only if the debtor demonstrates by clear and convincing evidence that the new case was filed in good faith as to the creditors to be stayed. The same presumption of bad faith applies as under Section 362(c)(3), and it is even harder to overcome because the debtor has a pattern of multiple dismissed cases.

Until the court grants the motion, there is no stay. The debtor has no automatic protection. This means that in the days or weeks between filing and the hearing on the motion, creditors are free to act. For this reason, debtors in this situation should file the motion to impose the stay simultaneously with the petition and request an emergency or expedited hearing.

Circuit Split: Does 362(c)(3) Terminate the Stay as to Estate Property?

There is a significant circuit split on whether Section 362(c)(3) terminates the stay only as to the debtor personally, or also as to property of the bankruptcy estate. The statute says the stay "shall terminate with respect to the debtor" after 30 days. Some courts read this literally - the stay terminates only as to the debtor, but remains in effect as to property of the estate. Other courts interpret the provision broadly to terminate the stay as to both the debtor and estate property.

This distinction matters enormously for homeowners. If the stay remains in effect as to property of the estate (the house), the lender still cannot foreclose even after 30 days. If the stay terminates as to everything, the lender can proceed. Check the law in your circuit before relying on either interpretation.

Interaction with Other Filing Bars

Repeat filers face multiple overlapping restrictions beyond the stay limitations:

Section 1328(f) - Bars discharge (not just the stay) for repeat Chapter 13 filers within 2 or 4 years of a prior filing, depending on the chapter of the prior case

Section 109(g) - Bars filing entirely for 180 days after a voluntary dismissal following a motion for relief from stay, or after a dismissal for willful failure to comply with court orders

Section 727(a)(8)/(a)(9) - Bars Chapter 7 discharge for 8 years after a prior Chapter 7 filing or 6 years after a prior Chapter 13 filing

A debtor who refiles within the Section 362(c)(3) or (c)(4) window may also face a discharge bar that makes the new filing pointless even if the stay is extended or imposed. The 1328(f) Discharge Screener can help determine whether a prior filing creates a discharge bar.

Related pages: Automatic Stay Overview - What the Stay Stops - Stay Violations - Relief from Stay Motions

Further Reading & Resources

Authority sources for deeper research on the automatic stay:

11 U.S.C. Section 362
Cornell LII - the statute that creates the automatic stay

11 U.S.C. Section 1301
Cornell LII - codebtor stay in Chapter 13

## Automatic Stay Exceptions - What Is Not Covered - 362 Stay
Source: https://automaticstay.org/stay-exceptions.html

Skip to main content

automaticstay.org
☰

Home
What It Stops
Violations
Repeat Filers
Relief from Stay
Exceptions
Duration
Stay Exceptions
Business vs Personal

Automatic Stay Exceptions - What Is Not Covered

Over 25 categories of actions that the automatic stay does not stop

Home › Stay Exceptions

Section 362(b) Exceptions

While the automatic stay is broad, it is not absolute. Section 362(b) of the Bankruptcy Code lists specific actions that are not stopped by the filing of a bankruptcy petition. Here are the most important ones:

Criminal Proceedings

The automatic stay does not stop criminal prosecutions. If you are facing criminal charges - even for writing bad checks or fraud - the criminal case proceeds regardless of your bankruptcy filing. The stay only applies to civil collection activity, not criminal law enforcement.

Domestic Support Obligations

Child support and alimony (domestic support obligations under Section 101(14A)) are not stayed. Actions to establish paternity, establish or modify a support order, collect support from property that is not part of the bankruptcy estate, and withhold income for support all continue unaffected.

This is one of the broadest exceptions. Even wage garnishment for child support or alimony continues through bankruptcy.

Tax Proceedings

Several tax-related actions are exempt from the stay:

Tax audits and assessments

Issuance of a notice of tax deficiency

Demand for tax returns

Making an assessment of any tax and issuing a notice and demand for payment

However, the IRS and state tax agencies cannot collect on tax debts during the stay (unless they get relief from stay). The exception allows auditing and assessment, but not collection.

Eviction Actions (Limited)

Under Section 362(b)(22), if the landlord obtained a judgment for possession of the property before the bankruptcy was filed, the eviction can proceed. The debtor can delay this by filing a certification and depositing rent with the court, but the window is narrow (30 days).

If no pre-petition eviction judgment exists, the stay does stop the eviction temporarily - though the landlord can quickly file for relief from stay.

Regulatory and Police Power Actions

Government actions to enforce regulatory or police powers are not stayed. This includes environmental enforcement, health and safety regulation, licensing actions, and similar governmental exercises of regulatory authority. The test is whether the government is acting in its regulatory capacity (not stayed) or as a creditor seeking to collect money (stayed).

Other Notable Exceptions

Pension loan setoff: Loans from retirement plans can continue to be repaid through payroll deduction

Presentment of negotiable instruments: Checks can still be deposited and processed

Securities regulation: SEC and CFTC enforcement actions

Certain setoffs: Setoffs by commodity brokers, forward contract merchants, and financial institutions under safe harbors

Cross-References

Exceptions overview

automaticstay.org - Full Section 362 guide

relieffromstay.org - When creditors can get the stay lifted

serialfiler.org - Repeat filer stay restrictions

Further Reading & Resources

Authority sources for deeper research on the automatic stay:

11 U.S.C. Section 362
Cornell LII - the statute that creates the automatic stay

11 U.S.C. Section 1301
Cornell LII - codebtor stay in Chapter 13

## Creditor Violated the Automatic Stay? What to Do - 362 Stay
Source: https://automaticstay.org/violations.html

Skip to main content

automaticstay.org
☰

Home
What It Stops
Violations
Repeat Filers
Relief from Stay
Exceptions

Creditor Violated the Automatic Stay?

What to do when a creditor ignores the bankruptcy filing and how to recover damages under 11 U.S.C. Section 362(k).

Home›Violations

Understanding Stay Violations

The automatic stay is not a suggestion - it is a federal court order that takes effect the instant a bankruptcy petition is filed. Any action by a creditor to collect a pre-petition debt, enforce a lien, or repossess property after the filing violates the stay and may be void or voidable.

Courts take stay violations seriously because the stay is fundamental to the bankruptcy process. Without it, the orderly administration of the estate - and the debtor's ability to reorganize or receive a fresh start - would be impossible.

Common Stay Violations

Continuing collection calls or sending demand letters after receiving notice of the filing

Repossessing a vehicle after the petition date

Proceeding with foreclosure when the lender had notice of the bankruptcy

Garnishing wages after the filing, or refusing to return garnished funds

Filing or continuing a lawsuit against the debtor for a pre-petition debt

Sending account statements with collection language or threats

Reporting the debt as delinquent to credit bureaus after the filing (in some circuits)

Offsetting bank account funds against a pre-petition loan balance

Refusing to reconnect utilities despite the stay and adequate assurance of payment

Section 362(k): Your Right to Damages

11 U.S.C. Section 362(k)(1) provides that an individual injured by any willful violation of the stay shall recover actual damages, including costs and attorney fees, and in appropriate circumstances, may recover punitive damages.

What Counts as "Willful"?

A violation is "willful" if the creditor knew of the stay and intentionally took the action that violated it. The creditor does not need to have intended to violate the law - only to have intended the act itself. In most circuits, if a creditor knew about the bankruptcy filing and still repossessed the vehicle, that is willful. The creditor's good faith belief that the action was permissible is generally not a defense.

Types of Damages Available

Actual damages - Out-of-pocket costs caused by the violation (towing fees, rental car costs, lost wages from missing work, bank fees, etc.)

Emotional distress damages - Available in many circuits when the violation causes significant distress, though some circuits require evidence beyond mere annoyance

Attorney fees and costs - The debtor can recover the cost of bringing the motion to enforce the stay

Punitive damages - Available when the creditor's conduct is egregious, in bad faith, or shows a pattern of disregard for the stay. Courts have awarded punitive damages ranging from a few thousand dollars to six figures in extreme cases

Important: Section 362(k) applies only to "individuals." Courts are split on whether business entities (corporations, LLCs) can recover damages under this section. Some courts allow business debtors to seek sanctions under Section 105(a) instead.

How to Document a Stay Violation

Strong documentation is critical to proving a stay violation and recovering damages. Start building your evidence the moment you become aware of the violation.

1. Preserve All Communications

Save every letter, email, voicemail, and text message from the creditor. Screenshot caller ID logs. If a debt collector calls, note the date, time, caller's name, and what was said. Do not delete voicemails - forward them to email if possible.

2. Document the Timeline

Create a written timeline showing: (1) when the bankruptcy was filed, (2) when the creditor received notice, (3) when each violating action occurred, and (4) what harm resulted. The time stamp on the bankruptcy petition is the legal start of the stay.

3. Confirm Notice Was Received

The bankruptcy court sends notice to all listed creditors, but there can be a delay of days or weeks. If your attorney (or you) sent separate notice to the creditor - by fax, email, or certified mail - retain that proof. Direct notice strengthens the "willful" element because the creditor cannot claim ignorance.

4. Calculate Your Damages

Gather receipts, bank statements, pay stubs, and any other evidence of financial harm. If you missed work because your car was repossessed, document the days missed and wages lost. If you paid for a rental car, keep the receipt. If the violation caused emotional distress, document the impact - medical records, prescriptions, or a written description of how the violation affected your daily life.

5. File a Motion for Contempt

The motion is filed in the bankruptcy court where your case is pending. It should describe the stay, the violation, the creditor's knowledge of the filing, and the damages suffered. Many districts have local forms. If you have an attorney, they will handle this. If you are pro se, check your court's website for motion templates and local rules on hearing procedures.

What Happens After You File the Motion

The court will typically set a hearing. The creditor must respond to the motion and may argue that the violation was not willful, that they had no notice, or that the stay did not apply (e.g., because an exception under Section 362(b) applied).

If the court finds a willful violation, it will order the creditor to take corrective action - returning property, reversing garnishments, ceasing collection activity - and award damages. The court may also refer the matter for additional sanctions under Section 105(a) or even report the conduct to regulatory authorities.

Some creditors settle before the hearing once they realize the violation is documented. The possibility of punitive damages and attorney fee-shifting creates a strong incentive for creditors to resolve violations quickly.

Void vs. Voidable Actions

Courts disagree on whether actions taken in violation of the stay are automatically void (as if they never happened) or merely voidable (valid until the court says otherwise). The majority rule in most circuits is that stay violations are void ab initio, meaning the action has no legal effect from the moment it occurs. Under this rule, a foreclosure sale conducted in violation of the stay conveys no title, regardless of whether the buyer or the lender knew about the bankruptcy.

The minority rule treats violations as voidable, giving the court discretion to validate the action in appropriate circumstances. This distinction matters most in real property transactions where innocent third-party buyers may be involved.

Related pages: What the Automatic Stay Stops - Exceptions to the Stay - Repeat Filer Limits - Section 362 on 1328f.com

Further Reading & Resources

Authority sources for deeper research on the automatic stay:

Cornell LII - the statute that creates the automatic stay

11 U.S.C. Section 1301
Cornell LII - codebtor stay in Chapter 13

## What the Automatic Stay Stops: Complete List - 362 Stay
Source: https://automaticstay.org/what-it-stops.html

Skip to main content

automaticstay.org
☰

Home
What It Stops
Violations
Repeat Filers
Relief from Stay
Exceptions

What the Automatic Stay Stops

A complete breakdown of what creditors can and cannot do once you file for bankruptcy under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Home›What It Stops

Actions the Automatic Stay Stops

Section 362(a) creates a broad injunction that halts nearly all creditor activity the moment a bankruptcy petition is filed. Below is a practical guide to the most common actions that are stayed, organized by category.

Foreclosure

All mortgage foreclosure proceedings are halted, whether judicial or non-judicial. A scheduled foreclosure sale must be canceled or postponed. Even if the sale was hours away at the time of filing, it cannot proceed. If a foreclosure sale occurs after the petition is filed but before the creditor learns of the filing, courts generally treat the sale as void. The debtor may need to file the case with enough lead time for the court to notify the creditor - but the legal effect is retroactive to the moment of filing. See Section 362(a)(1), (a)(3), (a)(4).

Vehicle Repossession

Auto lenders cannot repossess vehicles after the petition is filed. If a vehicle is repossessed before filing, the debtor may be able to compel turnover of the vehicle under Section 542, depending on the timing and jurisdiction. If a creditor repossesses a vehicle after the filing, that is a clear stay violation under Section 362(k), and the creditor must return the vehicle and may be liable for damages. This applies to all secured personal property - not just vehicles.

Wage Garnishment

All wage garnishments for pre-petition debts must stop. The debtor or debtor's attorney should notify the employer and the garnishing creditor of the bankruptcy filing. Any wages garnished after the petition date must be returned. State-law garnishment proceedings are stayed under Section 362(a)(1) and (a)(2). Note: garnishments for domestic support obligations (child support, alimony) are NOT stayed. See exceptions.

Lawsuits Against the Debtor

All pending civil lawsuits against the debtor are frozen. No new lawsuits can be filed. This includes breach of contract claims, personal injury lawsuits (with some exceptions), collection suits, and deficiency actions. The plaintiff in a pending lawsuit must be notified and the case is typically stayed by filing a "Suggestion of Bankruptcy" in the state or federal court where the lawsuit is pending. The lawsuit remains frozen until the bankruptcy court lifts the stay or the case closes.

Collection Calls and Letters

All collection activity must cease. This includes phone calls, letters, emails, text messages, and social media contacts. Debt collectors who continue calling after receiving notice of the filing violate both the automatic stay and potentially the Fair Debt Collection Practices Act. The debtor does not need to individually notify every creditor - the filing itself creates the stay - but providing written notice removes any defense that the violation was not "willful."

Utility Disconnections

Under Section 366, utility companies (electric, gas, water, phone) cannot disconnect service for 20 days after the petition is filed based on pre-petition debt. Within that 20-day period, the debtor must provide adequate assurance of future payment - typically a deposit equal to one month's estimated service. If the debtor provides adequate assurance, the utility cannot disconnect for the duration of the case. Post-petition utility bills must be paid as they come due.

Bank Account Levies and Freezes

Creditors cannot levy or freeze bank accounts after the petition is filed. If a creditor has already obtained a court order to seize funds but has not yet executed the levy, the stay prevents execution. If funds are frozen or seized after the filing, the creditor must release them. Banks sometimes freeze accounts upon learning of a bankruptcy filing to determine whether funds are property of the estate - this is distinct from a creditor levy and is generally permissible for a brief period.

Tax Collection

The IRS and state tax authorities cannot proceed with liens, levies, or seizures for pre-petition tax debts. Tax assessments can continue (this is an exception under Section 362(b)(9)), but active collection is stayed. The stay does not prevent the filing of a tax return or the issuance of a tax refund, but it does prevent the IRS from offsetting a refund against pre-petition debt in some circumstances. Tax liens that attached before the filing remain in place but cannot be enforced.

Eviction (Limited)

Eviction proceedings are stayed by the filing, but Section 362(b)(22) creates a significant exception: if the landlord obtained a judgment for possession before the petition was filed, the eviction may proceed despite the stay. The debtor can delay by filing a certification (and deposit) under Section 362(l) claiming the right to cure under applicable state law, but this provides only a 30-day window. In practice, the automatic stay provides much less protection for renters facing eviction than for homeowners facing foreclosure.

What the Stay Does NOT Stop

The following actions are specifically exempted from the automatic stay under Section 362(b). Creditors or other parties can continue these actions even while the bankruptcy case is pending.

Criminal Proceedings

The government can prosecute criminal cases regardless of a bankruptcy filing. This includes criminal fraud charges, DUI proceedings, and any other criminal prosecution. Section 362(b)(1).

Domestic Support Obligations

Collection of child support and alimony is not stayed. This includes wage withholding orders for support, contempt proceedings for unpaid support, and paternity and support establishment actions. Section 362(b)(2).

Certain Tax Proceedings

Tax audits, tax assessments, tax demands, and issuance of deficiency notices may continue. Active collection (levies, seizures) is stayed, but the assessment process is not. Section 362(b)(9).

Government Regulatory and Police Power

Government agencies can continue enforcing regulatory actions related to public health, safety, and welfare. This includes environmental enforcement, building code violations, and professional license proceedings. Section 362(b)(4).

Prior Eviction Judgments

If a landlord already has a judgment for possession before the filing, eviction can proceed under Section 362(b)(22). The debtor has a narrow cure right under Section 362(l) in some states, but it is limited to 30 days.

See the full list of exceptions under Section 362(b) →

Related resources: If a creditor has violated the stay, see What to Do About Stay Violations. For the intersection of repeat filings and stay protection, see Automatic Stay Limits for Repeat Filers. To check whether a prior filing affects your eligibility for discharge, use the free Section 1328(f) Discharge Screener.

## Privacy Policy - 362stay
Source: https://362stay/privacy.html

362stay

Home
Privacy

Privacy Policy

How we handle your data. Short version: we don't sell it, share it, or use it for advertising.

Data We Collect

We use Google Analytics (GA4) to measure aggregate site traffic. This includes page views, session duration, approximate geographic region (country and city level), and device type. We do not collect names, email addresses, or personally identifiable information through analytics.

Aggregate event data from Google Analytics is also exported to Google BigQuery for research use. The BigQuery data is used only for internal trajectory analysis and network health reports, is not shared with third parties, and is not used for advertising.

What We Don't Do

We do not sell data. We do not run advertising. We do not collect financial information. We do not use retargeting. We do not share any user data with attorneys, law firms, creditors, debt collectors, or legal service providers.

Specifically, we do not enable Google Signals, Google Ads integration, User-ID collection, or any cross-device tracking feature in Google Analytics. GA4 operates in aggregate-traffic-measurement mode only on this site.

Cookies

We use only first-party cookies set by Google Analytics for aggregate traffic measurement. We do not use advertising cookies, tracking pixels, or third-party analytics cookies.

Third-Party Services

Google Analytics (GA4) - aggregate traffic measurement

Google BigQuery - aggregate event data warehouse for research use only; not shared, not used for advertising

Google Search Console - search query measurement (clicks, impressions, our own page rankings)

GitHub Pages - hosting

Contact Forms & Email

This site may link to contact forms or email addresses operated by the Open Bankruptcy Project. Any data you submit through those channels is handled under the Open Bankruptcy Project master privacy policy, which governs all sites in this network.

Children's Privacy

This site is not directed at children under 13. We do not knowingly collect information from children.

Data Retention

Analytics data is retained for 14 months per Google's standard retention settings. After that window, individual event data is automatically deleted by Google; aggregate report metrics remain available.

Your Rights

You may request deletion of any personal data we hold by contacting info@openbankruptcyproject.org. California residents have additional rights under CCPA. EU residents have additional rights under GDPR. All such requests are handled by the Open Bankruptcy Project.

Network Policy

This site is part of the Bankruptcy Transparency Network operated by the Open Bankruptcy Project, a 501(c)(3) nonprofit. The same privacy standards apply across every site in the network. The authoritative master policy is maintained at https://openbankruptcyproject.org/privacy.html.

Changes

We may update this policy from time to time. Changes are reflected on the master policy first and then mirrored across all network sites. Check the master policy for the most current version.

Effective: March 30, 2026  |  Updated: April 11, 2026

Home  ·  Privacy  ·  Open Bankruptcy Project

Part of the Bankruptcy Transparency Network. Educational information only - not legal advice.

No ads. No affiliate links. No data sold. Supported by donations.
